name: Katyayana / Varttikakara
dates_notes: Kātyāyana is dated by relative position between Pāṇini and Patañjali; the vārttikas are known chiefly because Patañjali quotes and debates them in the Mahābhāṣya.
core_thesis: Kātyāyana is the grammarian remembered as the Vārttikakāra, the author of critical vārttikas on Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī. The vārttikas do not survive as a separately transmitted authorial book; they are embedded in Patañjali's Mahābhāṣya, where they function as proposals to supplement, restrict, or reinterpret Pāṇini's sūtras. Their philosophical importance lies in the method they establish for Sanskrit grammar: a sūtra is not merely recited but tested for over-application, under-application, economy, exception, and explanatory fit. This makes Kātyāyana a structural source for later Vyākaraṇa reflection on rule, usage, semantic expectation, and the relation between inherited śāstra and observed speech.

engaged_works.title: Varttikas on Panini's Ashtadhyayi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Kātyāyana; the text is preserved through Patañjali's Mahābhāṣya rather than as an independent recension.
engaged_works.summary: The vārttikas on Pāṇini are short critical observations, additions, restrictions, and interpretive proposals cited within the Mahābhāṣya. They supply a second-order grammar around the Aṣṭādhyāyī: when a rule needs an exception, when an affix requires restriction, when a formulation is too broad or too narrow, and when actual learned usage must be reconciled with sūtra economy. Because Patañjali regularly stages discussion around these vārttikas, the work is also a witness to an early scholastic method of grammatical argument. For the timeline, the important point is documentary rather than doctrinal overreach: Kātyāyana is the middle member of the Pāṇini-Kātyāyana-Patañjali grammatical triad, and his vārttikas are the form in which later Vyākaraṇa inherits the discipline of testing śāstra against usage and rule-coherence.
